2: pasan charan hegesasthe adelphoi mou hotan peirasmois peripesete poikilois (pāsan charān hēgēsasthe, adelphoi mou, hotan peirasmois peripesēte poikilois): 'Count it all joy when you fall into various trials.' Hegeomai (count/consider) is a deliberate act of valuation — joy is not a natural reaction to trials but a chosen re-evaluation. Peirasmoi (trials/temptations) covers both external testing by circumstances (James 1:2-4) and internal temptation by desire (James 1:13-14) — James distinguishes them but uses the same word, showing that external pressure and internal desire interact in the same human experience of testing.
22: ginesthe de poietai logou kai me monon akroatai paralogizomenoi heautous (ginesthe de poiētai logou kai mē monon akroatai paralogizomenoi heautous): 'Be doers of the word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.' Poietes logou (doer of the word): the verbal-moral contrast between hearing and doing is fundamental to both the wisdom tradition (Proverbs 2:1-6) and the dominical teaching (Matthew 7:24-27: the wise and foolish builders). Paralogizomenoi (deceiving, reasoning falsely alongside) implies a syllogistic self-deception: 'I heard the word, therefore I have received it and its benefits' — the error of intellectual reception without moral transformation.
1:
doulos — James identifies himself as a doulos (servant/slave) of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ. The term is more than a humility marker: in the OT, ebed YHWH (servant of the LORD) is a title of honor given to Moses (Deuteronomy 34:5), Joshua (Joshua 24:29), and the Servant of Isaiah (Isaiah 42:1; James 53:11). James, the brother of the Lord, adopts the Servant vocabulary rather than claiming familial status — a significant rhetorical and theological choice.

diaspora — the diaspora (scattered abroad) is the technical term for Jews living outside the land of Israel. James writes to tais dōdeka phylais tais en tē diasporā (the twelve tribes in the dispersion). Whether this is a literal address to Jewish-Christian congregations or a metaphorical address to the whole messianic community as God's scattered people (cf. 1 Peter 1:1) is debated; the twelve-tribes language makes a claim about the church as the reconstituted Israel.
3: dokimion — to dokimion hymōn tēs pisteōs (the testing of your faith). The noun dokimion is related to dokimos (approved/tested/genuine) and is used for the process of assaying metal to prove its genuineness. In 1 Peter 1:7, the same image appears: faith tested like gold in fire. The MKT translates it 'testing' rather than 'trial' to capture the assay-process meaning: the outcome is not merely hardship endured but faith proved genuine. The byproduct is hypomonē (endurance), not just survival.
4: teleios / holoklēros / leipomenoi — the verse stacks three Greek terms for completeness: teleios (mature/perfect/complete), holoklēros (whole/entire/intact — a term used of sacrificial animals with no missing parts), and the negation leipomenoi (lacking, from leipō). James wants hypomenē to complete its 'full work' (ergon teleion) so that the person may be teleios kai holoklēros — mature and intact, lacking nothing. The use of teleios at 1:4 picks up the same word from James 1:17 (perfect gift) and James 1:25 (perfect law); the word is thematic throughout James.
5: haplōs — God gives haplōs (generously/simply/without reservation). This rare adverb (haplos) is from haplos (single, simple, undivided) — the same root as haplous in Matthew 6:22 (the single/sound eye). God's giving is not divided, not begrudging, not complicated by conditions that vary from person to person. The contrast with the dipsychos (double-minded, two-souled) of v8 is deliberate: God is the single-minded giver; the doubter is the double-minded asker. Wisdom is available from God without the kind of internal division that characterizes the wavering person.
6: klydōni thalassēs anemizomenō kai rhipizomenō — the doubter is like a wave of the sea (klydōni — surge, billow) being anemizomenō (wind-driven) and rhipizomenō (tossed). Both participles are present tense, depicting continuous agitation. The sea-wave image for instability and lack of grounding is an OT wisdom image (cf. Isaiah 57:20: 'the wicked are like the tossing sea, which cannot rest'). James uses physical instability as the metaphor for the psychological/spiritual instability of the person who simultaneously asks and doubts whether the asking will work.
7: ekeinēs — 'that person' (ekeinēs) is demonstratively distancing — pointing to the doubter as a case to be considered from the outside. The double negative mē oiesthō hoti lēmpsetai ti (let them not suppose that they will receive anything) uses oiesthō (suppose/think) for a kind of wishful or unfounded expectation. The person who both asks and doubts has not made the decisive commitment to trust that prayer requires; they are hedging, and hedging prayer produces nothing.
8: dipsychos — one of the most discussed rare Greek words in the NT. Dipsychos (double-souled/double-minded) appears in the NT only in James (James 1:8; James 4:8). It may be James's own coinage or derived from Jewish wisdom tradition (cf. Testament of Asher 3:2 on the two-faced person). The compound di (two) + psychē (soul/self) depicts internal division — the person whose will is split between trust and doubt, between God and the world. The word becomes a technical term for wavering in early Christian literature (especially the Shepherd of Hermas), suggesting James introduced it into Christian vocabulary.
9: tapeinosis — the rich person should boast in their tapeinōsis (humiliation/low position). This is an inversion: wealth normally produces hypsos (height/honor); James says the rich should glory in their tapeinōsis, the exact word for the downward social trajectory that wealth loss brings. The reversal vocabulary is consistent with the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:48: epeblepsen epi tēn tapeinōsin tēs doulēs autou) — those who are humbled are exalted, and vice versa.
10: anthos chortou — anthos (blossom/flower) + chortos (grass/hay) — the same image used in Isaiah 40:6-8 ('all flesh is grass, and all its glory is like the flower of the field; the grass withers, the flower fades'). James is deliberately echoing Isaiah's great text on the transience of human power. The rich person will 'pass away' (maranthesetai, wither/fade) like the grass-flower: not merely die but be visibly reduced, the verb catching the visual process of wilting. The hot wind that withers in v11 is the kapson (scorching heat/sirocco wind) from the east — a detail of Levantine climate.
11: kapson — the scorching east wind (kapson; also in Matthew 20:12; Luke 12:55) is the sirocco wind that blows from the Arabian desert. It does not merely dry out; it shrivels living vegetation in hours. The phrase exēranen ton chorton kai to anthos autou exepesen (withered the grass and its flower fell off) describes a visible sudden end — the same speed that wealth can disappear. Euprепeia (beauty/good appearance) in 'its beauty perishes' is a hapax in the NT; it is the term for beautiful/noble appearance — the outward excellence that people associate with wealth and that the sirocco eliminates instantly.
12:
makarios — the beatitude form (blessed is the man who endures trial, makarios anēr hypomenei peirasmon) echoes Psalms 1:1 and the Beatitudes. Makarios in Greek has a range from 'happy' to 'blessed by God'; in wisdom literature it describes the state of the person who is rightly oriented toward God and his purposes.

stephanos tēs zōēs — the crown of life (stephanos = victor's wreath, not a royal crown which would be diadema). In the Greco-Roman world, stephanos was the athletic prize and the civic honor-crown. James uses the civic/athletic image but spiritualizes the recipient: the one who has loved God (agapōsin auton) — a participle describing ongoing disposition, not merely a past decision.
13: apeirastos kakōn — God 'cannot be tempted by evil' (apeirastos kakōn). The adjective apeirastos is a hapax in the NT and rare in Greek literature. It is likely passive in meaning ('not subject to being tempted/tested by evil') rather than active ('unable to test with evil'). The point is not that God lacks power to test but that evil has no purchase on God — he has no internal inclination toward it that could be exploited. This is the ground for the argument of v14: since God himself is not inclined toward evil, any evil inclination must have its origin in the human person.
14: epithymias / deleazomenos — each person is tempted when 'drawn away' (exelkomenos — dragged out from a place of safety) and 'enticed' (deleazomenos — the latter word is a fishing term meaning 'lured with bait'; cf. 2 Peter 2:14, 18 where it appears for those who entice new converts). The personification of epithymia (desire/lust/craving) as an active agent that draws and baits the person is vivid: desire is not portrayed as passive but as a hunter. The origin is internal (hypo tēs idias epithymias, by one's own desire) — emphatically one's own.
15: syllabousa... apotiktei... apotelesthēisa — a sequence of three aorist participles building a biological metaphor of conception, birth, and maturity: desire conceives (syllabousa, lit. 'having taken together' — the technical term for conception), gives birth to sin (apotiktei), and sin, when fully grown (apotelesthēisa, having reached its full end/maturity), gives birth to death. The chain is methodical: desire → sin → death, echoing the death-pattern established in Genesis 2:17. The word apotelesthēisa (completed/come to full term) is rare; it catches the idea of sin reaching its terminal maturity.
16: planasthe — do not be deceived (mē planāsthe, present passive imperative) frames what follows as a warning against a common error of thinking. The verb planaō (wander/be led astray) is the root of planē (error/deception). The command is to stop being led astray — implying the tendency is already present. The contrast that follows (v17) is the truth that corrects the error: the character of God as giver of good rather than source of temptation.
17: aposkiasma tropēs — one of the most beautiful phrases in James: God is the Father of lights with whom there is parallagē ē tropēs aposkiasma — no variation or shadow due to turning. Parallagē (variation/alteration) is an astronomical term for the apparent change in a celestial body's position; tropē (turning) is used for the solstice (the sun's 'turning' point); aposkiasma (shadow from) is the shadow produced by the eclipse or the movement of a heavenly body. James uses astronomical precision to describe divine constancy: the Father of the heavenly lights (sun, moon, stars) is himself not subject to the variations those lights undergo. His character and giving do not shift like a shadow.
18: aparchē — James describes believers as aparchēn tina tōn autou ktismatōn (a kind of firstfruits of his creatures). The term aparchē (firstfruits) is the technical Levitical term for the first and representative portion of a harvest offered to God, which consecrates the whole (Leviticus 23:10; Numbers 15:20-21; Romans 11:16). By calling believers 'firstfruits,' James places the church within the redemptive-harvest framework: they are the first installment of God's new-creation harvest, with the implication that more will follow. The 'word of truth' by which he gave them birth echoes the creation-by-word motif (Gen 1; Psalms 33:6) applied to new creation.
19: tachy... bradys... bradys — the famous triad: quick to hear (tachy eis to akouein), slow to speak (bradys eis to lalein), slow to anger (bradys eis orgēn). The Greek word order highlights the contrast: two 'quicks' and one 'slow' would make the typical person's natural tendency the opposite of what James commands. The asymmetry (one fast, two slow) teaches the priority of listening. The command is set up by v18's 'word of truth': having been 'given birth' by that word, the appropriate posture toward all words is careful reception before rapid production.
20: orgē andros — human anger (orgē andros, literally 'man's wrath' — anēr is the male-specific noun rather than the generic anthrōpos) does not accomplish (ergazetai, work/produce) the dikaiosynēn theou (righteousness of God). The phrase 'righteousness of God' could mean the righteousness God requires, or the righteous state God produces, or God's own righteousness. In this context it likely means 'the righteous conduct that God desires' — human anger is counterproductive to the community life James envisions. The andros specificity may be a realistic observation about where anger was most commonly exercised.
21: emphyton logon — accept the implanted word (ton emphyton logon dexasthe). The adjective emphytos (implanted/innate) appears only here in the NT. It is used in Greek philosophy for inborn knowledge; here James applies it to the word of truth (v18) that has been planted/implanted in the believer through the new birth. The contrast is with filth (rJyparia) and surplus of evil (perisseia kakias) that is to be removed. The image suggests the word is not external information but seed already present within, waiting to be cultivated by the humble (prautēti, meekness/gentleness) reception of it.
23: en esoptrō — the mirror (esoptron) metaphor is used to describe the hearer-only who looks at their natural face (prosōpon tēs genesēos autou, literally 'the face of his birth/origin') in a mirror. The mirror in the ancient world was polished bronze — not a glass mirror — and its image was imperfect and fleeting. The image of seeing one's face in a mirror was proverbial for self-examination; James's point is that the examination that does not lead to change is as transient as a glance that produces no retention.
24: katenoēsen kai apelelythen — he looked (katanoeō, attentively observed/considered) and has gone away (aperchomai, departed). The sequence of aorists (both action and departure) emphasizes the brevity: the looking and the going away happen rapidly without any lasting effect. The perfect epelēlathen (has forgotten) indicates a current state resulting from past action: the forgetting has already taken hold and persists. The point is not that careful observation is worthless but that observation without formation produces self-deception.
25:
nomos teleios tēs eleutherias — the 'perfect law of liberty/freedom' (nomos teleios tēs eleutherias). All three words are loaded: nomos (law) used here is not Torah in the Pauline polemical sense but instruction/principle; teleios (perfect/mature/complete) picks up the theme from James 1:4; eleutheria (freedom) is a term with strong Stoic associations (the truly free person is the one who acts consistently with reason) but James uses it for freedom from the disordered desires described in vv14-15. The 'perfect law of liberty' is the implanted word (v21) that, when obeyed, produces genuine freedom rather than the servitude of desire.

parakyptein — looks intently (parakyptein) — the same verb used in 1 Peter 1:12 for angels stooping to look into the gospel mysteries. It describes a careful, attentive bending over to examine something closely — the exact opposite of the mirror-glance of v23-24.
26: thrēskos / thrēskeia — religion (thrēskeia) and religious (thrēskos) are rare words in the NT (only here in James and in Acts 26:5; Colossians 2:18). The word-group denotes the outward practice of religion — rites, ceremonies, forms of devotion. James uses it here and in v27 to make a sharp distinction: religion that does not control the tongue is worthless (mataios, empty/futile/vain), regardless of outward piety. The irony is that thrēskeia normally referred to careful cultic observance; James inverts it: the truly careful religious person controls speech and serves the vulnerable.
27: katharos kai amiantos — the pure and undefiled religion: katharos (clean/pure — the standard cultic purity term) and amiantos (undefiled/unstained — used in Hebrews 7:26 for Jesus as the perfect high priest). James uses the Levitical purity vocabulary to describe not ritual cleanliness but the social righteousness of caring for orphans and widows. The 'world' from which one keeps oneself 'unstained' (aspilon) is not physical creation but the world-system of disordered values. Three purity terms in one verse redirect the concept of ritual cleanness toward relational and ethical cleanness.
